# RFC-0009: Runtime Time-Bound Semantics

> **Version:** 0.2.1 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0009:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0009c-summary"></a>

This RFC defines one time-bound model for InferLab runtime operations: an operator or resolved policy supplies a duration, one owning operation establishes one elapsed-time budget, nested attempts consume its remaining time, and lifecycle cleanup begins only after the operation has reached a terminal outcome.

**Scope:** The model applies to finite and deliberately unbounded runtime waits, including server readiness, integration and image probes, profiler control, Eval and Bench cases, external measurement clients, and cleanup verification. It distinguishes operation budgets from per-attempt caps, cleanup grace, polling cadence, progress reporting, and transport liveness. It does not require arbitrary wall-clock limits for installation, builds, capture-armed readiness, or another workflow whose governing contract deliberately leaves it unbounded. It does not introduce a general workflow language or operation graph.

**Rationale:** Existing paths reuse duration values at multiple nested enforcement points. A duration can consequently mean an end-to-end budget in one layer, a single network-attempt cap in another, and a process timeout in a third. Naming one authority and requiring nested work to consume remaining time prevents those layers from extending, racing, or preempting the operator-visible bound.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0009:C-OPERATION-BUDGETS] Operation Budgets And Deadlines (Normative) <a id="rfc-0009c-operation-budgets"></a>

Every configured runtime timeout MUST identify the concrete operation whose elapsed time it bounds and the event at which that budget begins.

A finite runtime operation MUST have exactly one control-plane authority that establishes its deadline from the frozen resolved duration when the operation reaches its declared start event.

The owning authority MUST classify the operation as timed out when that deadline expires before another terminal outcome is accepted.

A nested step MUST consume the owning operation's remaining budget and MUST NOT establish a fresh copy of the original duration.

A bounded single attempt, including a connection, request, probe, child-process wait, or external-tool guard, MUST use no more than the lesser of its own attempt cap and the owning operation's remaining budget.

A subordinate attempt MAY fail before the owning operation's deadline when its effective attempt bound expires. That attempt failure MUST NOT by itself classify the owning operation as timed out; the owning authority decides whether the operation retries, fails for that attempt error, or continues through another supported path.

A downstream native timeout or startup guard MUST NOT classify the owning InferLab operation as timed out and MUST NOT extend its deadline. InferLab MAY disable that downstream guard when the owning control-plane deadline can terminate and clean up the complete owned process group.

An operation governed as unbounded MUST remain terminable by every non-time terminal condition required by its owning workflow, including process exit and operator interruption where they apply.

Polling cadence, retry backoff, progress-report timing, and transport keepalive MUST NOT be treated or recorded as operation budgets.

An operation that is intentionally unbounded MUST NOT acquire an arbitrary finite deadline solely because it invokes an external command.

**Rationale:** One deadline is the authority for one operation, while attempt caps keep individual I/O or tool calls responsive inside that authority. An attempt cap may produce an earlier, accurately classified attempt failure without becoming a second operation-timeout authority. Explicit unbounded operation semantics preserve workflows such as instrumented startup and package installation where an invented wall-clock limit would convert legitimate progress into false failure.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0009:C-MEASUREMENT-CASE-BUDGETS] Measurement Case Budgets (Normative) <a id="rfc-0009c-measurement-case-budgets"></a>

For an Eval or Bench definition, `timeout_seconds` MUST be the end-to-end elapsed-time budget of each individual measurement case.

A measurement-case budget MUST begin immediately before the first case-owned action is permitted after admission and non-effectful preflight. The first action is prefix-cache reset when a Bench case requests it; otherwise it is the built-in endpoint request or release of the external measurement client to begin tokenizer loading, endpoint probing, or native-engine execution.

The same measurement-case budget MUST cover every later case-owned probe, request, native-engine execution, and result publication needed for the InferLab control plane to accept a successful or failed business result.

All trials requested by one repeated Eval definition MUST remain one measurement case under that single budget. Task and tokenizer preparation, endpoint probes when applicable, every released trial request, incremental result evidence, aggregate pass-rate calculation, and gate adjudication MUST consume that budget. A repeated Eval MUST NOT create a fresh operation deadline for each trial.

An Eval or Bench runtime MUST NOT grant the tokenizer, endpoint probe, native engine, individual model request, or result wrapper a fresh copy of the case duration.

The InferLab control plane MUST remain the authority that accepts a client result or classifies timeout, interruption, process failure, and cleanup outcome for an external measurement case under [RFC-0003:C-RUNTIME-WORKFLOWS](../rfc/RFC-0003.md#rfc-0003c-runtime-workflows) and [RFC-0006:C-INTEGRATIONS](../rfc/RFC-0006.md#rfc-0006c-integrations).

A measurement runtime MAY use the remaining case budget to bound a native operation so that it can publish more specific evidence before the control-plane deadline. That native bound MUST NOT extend the case deadline or replace the control plane's terminal authority.

A measurement case's business result MUST become terminal when the control plane accepts its result or classifies its failure, timeout, or interruption. Attached profiler stop, collection finalization, and report-coverage verification MUST occur after that case terminal point and MUST NOT consume the measurement-case budget.

Attached profiling MAY independently cause the overall profiled measurement or capture record to fail under [RFC-0004:C-WORKLOAD-PROFILING](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-workload-profiling). That later profiling outcome MUST preserve the case business result and MUST NOT reclassify the case as timed out.

Manual and closed-loop execution MUST use the same measurement-case budget start, consumption, timeout, and cleanup semantics for the same effective definition under [RFC-0004:C-MEASUREMENTS](../rfc/RFC-0004.md#rfc-0004c-measurements).

**Rationale:** Eval and Bench definitions expose one operator timeout, so the observable case must have one budget. Treating that value as both a whole-case duration and a fresh per-request or per-process duration makes sequential probes exceed it and causes nested supervisors to race over the terminal result. Profiling observes the case but has its own finalization and report authority, so its post-case lifecycle can fail the containing profiled workflow without rewriting the case's time-bound outcome.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0009:C-CLEANUP-GRACE] Cleanup Grace And Terminal Evidence (Normative) <a id="rfc-0009c-cleanup-grace"></a>

An operation's cleanup MUST begin only after that operation has reached success, failure, timeout, interruption, or cancellation.

Time spent terminating process groups, removing containers, stopping profiler sessions, reaping children, or verifying absence MUST NOT count toward an Eval or Bench measurement-case budget.

A cleanup stage that can block on an external process, daemon, network operation, or lifecycle verification MUST have its own explicitly named finite grace or verification deadline so cleanup cannot hang indefinitely. An immediate cleanup action MUST NOT acquire an arbitrary delay solely to fit that model.

A cleanup grace MUST NOT extend the preceding operation deadline, defer its timeout classification, or convert its terminal outcome into success.

A cleanup failure MAY cause the containing workflow or record to fail when its governing lifecycle requires verified cleanup. That failure MUST preserve the preceding operation's terminal cause rather than reclassifying it.

When one cleanup action invokes nested attempts, those attempts MUST consume the remaining cleanup deadline under [RFC-0009:C-OPERATION-BUDGETS](../rfc/RFC-0009.md#rfc-0009c-operation-budgets).

Runtime evidence for a finite operation MUST preserve its configured budget, declared start boundary, elapsed time, and terminal cause.

Runtime evidence for a deliberately unbounded operation MUST preserve that it was unbounded and its non-time terminal cause.

When cleanup is attempted, evidence MUST separately preserve its trigger, elapsed time, configured grace or deadline where one applies, termination and removal actions, verification result, and failure context.

Attempt evidence whose timeout affects diagnosis MUST distinguish its effective attempt bound from the owning operation budget.

**Rationale:** Cleanup is mandatory lifecycle work after the business outcome, not hidden capacity in that business operation. Recording the two intervals separately keeps operator expectations truthful while still bounding cleanup paths that can themselves wedge. A cleanup or profiling failure can truthfully fail the containing workflow without erasing the already-established business result or inventing a later timeout for it.

*Since: v0.1.0*
